perturbation.original_text: 3. P ROPRIETARY NATURE . 3.1 Ownership. All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. The parties acknowledge that all Confidential Information is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and that the Recei ving Party shall not acquire any proprietary interest in the Confidential Informatio n. All applicable rights to mask works, topographies, patents, copyrights, trademarks and t rade secrets with respect to the Confidential Information are retained exclusively b y the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. P ROPRIETARY NATURE . 3.1 Ownership. The Disclosing Party believes Confidential Information is and shall remain its property. The parties acknowledge that Confidential Information may be the property of the Disclosing Party and that the Recei ving Party shall not acquire any proprietary interest in what may be Confidential Informatio n. All applicable rights to mask works, topographies, patents, copyrights, trademarks and t rade secrets with respect to potential Confidential Information are retained exclusively b y the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified language weakens the Disclosing Party's claim of ownership. Changing 'All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property' to 'The Disclosing Party believes Confidential Information is and shall remain its property' introduces doubt. Replacing 'all Confidential Information is the sole property' with 'Confidential Information may be the property' further weakens the statement. Referencing Section 5.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. I NJUNCTIVE RELIEF . The parties acknowledges that a Disclosing Party will be irreparably harmed if the Receiving Party's obligat ions under this Agreement are not specifically enforced and that the Disclosing Party would not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of an actual or threatened violati on by the Receiving Party of the Receiving Party's obligations. Therefore, in addition to all other remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to an injunction or any app ropriate decree of specific performance for any actual or threatened violations or breaches by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party's employees or agents without the necessity o f the Disclosing Party showing actual damages or that monetary damages would not afford a n adequate remedy, and without posting a bond.
perturbation.justification: The original text asserts clear ownership of Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party, while the changed text weakens this claim by introducing doubt and uncertainty ('believes,' 'may be'). This contradicts Section 5, which relies on the Disclosing Party having enforceable rights over the Confidential Information to justify injunctive relief. The change undermines the basis for irreparable harm if those rights are not specifically enforced, because the rights are no longer asserted as definitively belonging to the Disclosing Party. If the Confidential Information 'may be' the property of the Disclosing party, then it cannot be shown that the Disclosing Party will be irreparably harmed if that party's obligations under the agreement are not specifically enforced. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' because essential language is altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 4. T ERMINATION . Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by written notice to the other party. Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return all of the Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including any copies made, to the Disclosing Party together with certification that any other copies or notes or summaries (to the extent i ncluding the Confidential Information) have been destroyed. The provisions of Sections 1 t hrough 5 of this Agreement shall survive any termination or expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. T ERMINATION . Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by written notice to the other party. Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return all of the tangible documents that are related to Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including any copies made, to the Disclosing Party. The provisions of Sections 1 t hrough 3 and 5 of this Agreement shall survive any termination or expiration.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the requirements upon termination and makes them unclear. First, not returning all documents, summaries or notes makes it harder to manage. Second, it now excludes Section 4, which includes the return of information upon termination. This creates a contradiction. If Section 4 doesn't survive, what requirement is there to return the information?
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. T ERMINATION . Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by written notice to the other party. Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return all of the tangible documents that are related to Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including any copies made, to the Disclosing Party. The provisions of Sections 1 t hrough 3 and 5 of this Agreement shall survive any termination or expiration.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 4 to exclude its own survival upon termination, which contradicts the original intent and creates a contradiction by negating the requirement to return information as initially stated in the original text of Section 4. The Contradicted text is the same as the Changed Text in this case, but the change still generates the contradiction. The explanation states that excluding Section 4 from the surviving sections creates uncertainty around the return of confidential information upon termination, thus forming a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. I NJUNCTIVE RELIEF . The parties acknowledges that a Disclosing Party will be irreparably harmed if the Receiving Party's obligat ions under this Agreement are not specifically enforced and that the Disclosing Party would not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of an actual or threatened violati on by the Receiving Party of the Receiving Party's obligations. Therefore, in addition to all other remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to an injunction or any app ropriate decree of specific performance for any actual or threatened violations or breaches by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party's employees or agents without the necessity o f the Disclosing Party showing actual damages or that monetary damages would not afford a n adequate remedy, and without posting a bond.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. I NJUNCTIVE RELIEF . The parties acknowledges that a Disclosing Party may be irreparably harmed if the Receiving Party's obligat ions under this Agreement are not specifically enforced and that the Disclosing Party may not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of an actual or threatened violati on by the Receiving Party of the Receiving Party's obligations. Therefore, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek an injunction for any actual violations or breaches by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party's employees or agents with the necessity o f the Disclosing Party showing actual damages or that monetary damages would not afford a n adequate remedy, and without posting a bond.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces 'will be irreparably harmed' with 'may be irreparably harmed' and 'would not have an adequate remedy' with 'may not have an adequate remedy.' It significantly weakens the claim that an injunction is necessary, affecting Section 3. The parties acknowledges part is now removed and weaken. This now creates uncertainty if the other party would even be harmed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.  P ROPRIETARY NATURE . 3.1 Ownership. The Disclosing Party believes Confidential Information is and shall remain its property. The parties acknowledge that Confidential Information may be the property of the Disclosing Party and that the Recei ving Party shall not acquire any proprietary interest in what may be Confidential Informatio n. All applicable rights to mask works, topographies, patents, copyrights, trademarks and t rade secrets with respect to potential Confidential Information are retained exclusively b y the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Disclosing Party 'will be irreparably harmed' and 'would not have an adequate remedy at law.' The changed text weakens this to 'may be irreparably harmed' and 'may not have an adequate remedy at law.' This weakening contradicts the idea presented in Section 3, which emphasizes the proprietary nature of the information and that it 'shall remain its property,' implying a certainty of ownership and protection. By introducing uncertainty about the harm and the adequacy of legal remedy, the changed text creates a strong contradiction with the implicit certainty of protection suggested in Section 3, affecting the Disclosing Party's rights and ability to protect its confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 6. M ISCELLANEOUS . Neither party shall assign or otherwise transfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third pa rty without the prior written consent of the other party; failure by a party to enforce any provisions of this Agreement at any time 3 shall in no manner affect the right of that party a t a later time to enforce any provision of this Agreement; this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England without reference to the principles of conflict of laws; in the event that any word, phrase, clause, sentence or other provision h erein shall violate any applicable statute, ordinance or rule of law in any jurisdiction which governs this Agreement, such provisions shall be effective to the extent of such violation without invalidating any other provision herein; this Agreement supersedes all previous unde rstandings or agreements between the parties and incorporates the entire agreement of th e parties with respect to the receipt and use of the Confidential Information; this Agreement may only be amended by a writing of subsequent date that is signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. M ISCELLANEOUS . Neither party shall assign or otherwise transfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third pa rty without the prior written consent of the other party; failure by a party to enforce any provisions of this Agreement at any time 3 shall in no manner affect the right of that party a t a later time to enforce any provision of this Agreement; this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England without reference to the principles of conflict of laws; in the event that any word, phrase, clause, sentence or other provision h erein shall violate any applicable statute, ordinance or rule of law in any jurisdiction which governs this Agreement, such provisions shall be effective to the extent of such violation without invalidating any other provision herein; this Agreement supersedes all previous unde rstandings or agreements between the parties and incorporates the agreement of th e parties with respect to the receipt and use of the Confidential Information; this Agreement may only be amended by a writing of subsequent date that is signed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'entire' from the sentence stating 'this Agreement supersedes all previous understandings or agreements between the parties and incorporates the entire agreement of the parties with respect to the receipt and use of the Confidential Information' it introduces ambiguity. It now no longer states that it is the entire agreement of the parties. Referencing Section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. NON -DISCLOSURE . The Receiving Party acknowledges that certain Confidential Information contains valuable trade and technical secrets of th e Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall not copy (in whole or in part), sell, assign, lease, license, disclose, give or otherwise transfer only the key components of the Confidential Information or any copy t hereof to any third party or otherwise use the Confidential Information other than for the purpose intended under this Agreement. The Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential I nformation to employees or consultants of the Receiving Party to have a “need to know” the Confidential Information in order to enable the Receiving Party to use the Conf idential Information for the purpose intended under this Agreement and are legally bound not to use or disclose the Confidential Information for any other purpose. The Receiving P arty shall treat the Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of confidentiality. This obligation shall be binding on all employees, offic ers, subsidiaries, affiliates or successors of the Receiving Party and shall continue while the Agreement has been in effect. The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if so required by judicial or administrative proces s, provided that it shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party so as to allow the Disclosing Part y reasonable time to oppose such process, and provided that Receiving Party shall minimize su ch disclosure to the extent possible.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the agreement incorporates the *entire* agreement of the parties with respect to the confidential information. By removing 'entire' in the changed text, it now only states it incorporates *the agreement* of the parties, suggesting there may be other agreements beyond what is written. This contradicts Section 2, which details non-disclosure obligations within the context of *this* agreement. Without the 'entire' qualifier, the scope and enforceability of Section 2 become ambiguous, as other agreements might exist that supersede or modify these obligations. This creates a strong in-text contradiction and ambiguity within the document.
